name: Operand Stack
summary: Scan the tokens left to right. Push each number onto a stack; on an operator, pop the top two values (the deeper one is the left operand), apply the operation, and push the result back. The single value left is the answer.
visualizationIntent: init (L3-4): an empty stack, ready to scan the tokens left to right. loop (L5): read the current token. check (L6,9,11,13): decide whether the token is an operator and, if so, which one. update-number (L19-20): a numeric token is converted and pushed onto the stack (the stack grows by one). update-operator (L7-8,10-16,17): the top two values are popped (stack shrinks by two) — b is the top / right operand, a is the deeper / left operand — the operation is applied, and the single result is pushed back (stack grows by one). return (L21-22): after every token, exactly one value remains on the stack — that value is the answer.

lineExplanations.3: Create an empty stack that will hold operands (numbers) waiting to be combined.
lineExplanations.4: The set of the four valid operator symbols, used to tell operators from numbers.
lineExplanations.5: Scan the tokens left to right, one at a time.
lineExplanations.6: Check whether the current token is an operator rather than a number.
lineExplanations.7: Pop the top value off the stack — this is b, the right-hand operand.
lineExplanations.8: Pop the next value — this is a, the left-hand (deeper) operand.
lineExplanations.10: Add the two operands: a + b.
lineExplanations.12: Subtract: a - b (the deeper operand a comes first).
lineExplanations.16: The remaining case is division; divide and truncate toward zero with int(a / b).
lineExplanations.17: Push the computed result back onto the stack so later operators can use it.
lineExplanations.19: The token is a number, so convert the string to an integer.
lineExplanations.20: Push that number onto the stack.
lineExplanations.21: After every token is processed, exactly one value remains — read it as the answer.
lineExplanations.22: Return the value of the whole expression.

syntaxExplanations.1: class Solution: — the class wrapper the platform expects your method to live inside.
syntaxExplanations.2: def evalRPN(self, tokens): — tokens is the list of operand and operator strings.
syntaxExplanations.3: stack = [] — an empty Python list used as a stack (.append pushes, .pop removes the last item).
syntaxExplanations.4: operators = {"+", "-", "*", "/"} — a set literal of the four operator symbols for fast membership tests.
syntaxExplanations.5: for token in tokens: — loop over the tokens, binding token to each in turn.
syntaxExplanations.6: if token in operators: — membership test; True when the token is one of the four operators.
syntaxExplanations.7: b = stack.pop() — remove and return the top of the stack into b.
syntaxExplanations.8: a = stack.pop() — remove and return the new top into a.
syntaxExplanations.9: if token == "+": — compare the token string to the plus sign.
syntaxExplanations.10: value = a + b — addition of the two popped operands.
syntaxExplanations.11: elif token == "-": — otherwise test for the minus sign.
syntaxExplanations.12: value = a - b — subtraction, left operand minus right.
syntaxExplanations.13: elif token == "*": — otherwise test for the multiply sign.
syntaxExplanations.14: value = a * b — multiplication of the two operands.
syntaxExplanations.15: else: — the only remaining case, division.
syntaxExplanations.16: value = int(a / b) — true division, then int() truncates toward zero as the problem requires.
syntaxExplanations.17: stack.append(value) — push the computed result back onto the stack.
syntaxExplanations.18: else: — the branch taken when the token was not an operator (a number).
syntaxExplanations.19: value = int(token) — convert the numeric string to an integer.
syntaxExplanations.20: stack.append(value) — push the number onto the stack.
syntaxExplanations.21: answer = stack[0] — the single value left on the stack is the result.
syntaxExplanations.22: return answer — hand the expression's value back to the caller.
